sections.title: Unix/Linux Overview
sections.content:
Unix is a family of operating systems that originated in the 1970s and became the foundation for modern Linux. Linux is a free, open-source operating system kernel created by Linus Torvalds in 1991. It powers servers, desktops, mobile phones, and embedded systems worldwide.

Key principles of Unix philosophy:
• Everything is a file (regular files, directories, devices, and processes)
• Do one thing and do it well (simple, focused tools)
• Programs should work together and handle text streams
• POSIX standard ensures compatibility across Unix-like systems

Linux distributions (Ubuntu, Fedora, CentOS, Debian, etc.) are different packaging of the Linux kernel with various tools and utilities. The shell is your interface to the operating system - it interprets your commands and communicates with the kernel.

Common shells: Bash (Bourne Again Shell), Zsh, Fish, Ksh. Most Linux systems use Bash as the default shell.

sections.title: Essential Shell Commands
sections.content:
Basic file and directory operations:

pwd - Print current working directory
cd [path] - Change directory (cd .. goes up one level, cd ~ goes to home)
ls - List directory contents (ls -l shows details, ls -a shows hidden files)
mkdir [name] - Create a new directory
rmdir [name] - Remove an empty directory
rm [file] - Delete a file (rm -r deletes directory and contents)
cp [source] [destination] - Copy files or directories (cp -r for recursive)
mv [source] [destination] - Move or rename files and directories
touch [file] - Create an empty file or update timestamp
cat [file] - Display file contents
more/less [file] - View file contents page by page
head [file] - Show first 10 lines
tail [file] - Show last 10 lines
find [path] [options] - Search for files
which [command] - Locate a command executable
type [command] - Show what type of command it is
echo [text] - Print text to output
wc [file] - Count words, lines, characters in a file

sections.title: File Permissions
sections.content:
Every file and directory has permissions that control who can read, write, and execute it. Permissions are shown as rwx (read, write, execute) for three categories: owner, group, others.

Permission representation: rwxrwxrwx
• First rwx: permissions for owner (file creator)
• Second rwx: permissions for group (users in the file's group)
• Third rwx: permissions for others (all other users)

Permission meanings:
• r (read, 4): ability to read file or list directory contents
• w (write, 2): ability to modify file or create/delete items in directory
• x (execute, 1): ability to run file as program or enter directory

Common commands:
chmod [permissions] [file] - Change permissions (chmod 755 file or chmod u+x file)
chown [user:group] [file] - Change owner and group
ls -l [file] - Display file permissions

Numeric notation: each digit is sum of r(4)+w(2)+x(1)
Example: chmod 755 means rwxr-xr-x (owner: full access, group and others: read and execute only)

Common permission sets:
644 - Text files (owner reads/writes, others read only)
755 - Executable files or directories (owner full, others read and execute)
700 - Private files (owner only)
777 - Full access for everyone (rarely used, security risk)

sections.title: Text Processing Commands
sections.content:
grep [pattern] [file] - Search for text patterns in files
• grep -i: case-insensitive search
• grep -v: show lines that DON'T match
• grep -n: show line numbers
• grep -c: count matching lines
• grep -r: search recursively in directories

sed [options] [file] - Stream editor for text transformation
• sed 's/old/new/' file: replace first occurrence on each line
• sed 's/old/new/g' file: replace all occurrences globally
• sed '2d' file: delete second line
• sed -n '5,10p' file: print lines 5-10 only

awk [options] [file] - Process and analyze text data
• awk '{print $1}' file: print first field
• awk -F: '{print $1}' file: use colon as field separator
• awk '{sum+=$1} END {print sum}' file: sum values in first field
• awk 'NR>1' file: print all lines except first

cut [options] [file] - Extract columns or fields
• cut -c 1-5 file: extract characters 1-5
• cut -d: -f1 file: extract first field using colon as delimiter

sort [file] - Sort lines alphabetically or numerically
• sort -n: numeric sort
• sort -r: reverse sort
• sort -k2: sort by second column

uniq [file] - Remove or report duplicate lines (works on sorted data)
tr [old] [new] - Translate or delete characters
diff [file1] [file2] - Show differences between files
wc [options] [file] - Count lines, words, or characters

sections.title: Piping, Redirection & I/O
sections.content:
Piping (|): Connect output of one command as input to another
Example: cat file.txt | grep 'error' | wc -l (find and count lines with 'error')

Redirection:
> - Redirect output to a file (overwrites existing content)
>> - Append output to a file (adds to end)
< - Redirect input from a file
2> - Redirect error output to a file
&> - Redirect both output and errors to a file

Examples:
echo 'Hello' > file.txt (create file with content)
ls -la >> results.txt (append directory listing to file)
grep 'error' input.txt > output.txt (save grep results to file)
command 2> errors.log (save errors to file)
command &> all.log (save output and errors to same file)

Standard streams:
• stdin (0): standard input from keyboard
• stdout (1): standard output to screen
• stderr (2): standard error to screen

/dev/null: special file that discards all data written to it
Example: command 2>/dev/null (hide error messages)

Combining pipes and redirections:
cat file.txt | grep 'pattern' | sort | uniq > unique_results.txt
ls *.txt 2>/dev/null | wc -l (count txt files, hide errors)

sections.title: Process Management
sections.content:
A process is a running instance of a program. Each process has a unique Process ID (PID).

ps - List running processes
• ps aux: show all running processes with detailed info
• ps -ef: another way to show all processes
• ps -u username: show processes for specific user
• ps | grep [name]: find process by name

top - Display real-time system activity and processes
• Shows CPU usage, memory usage, running processes
• Press 'q' to quit
• Shows load average and which processes use most resources

kill [PID] - Terminate a process
• kill 1234: send SIGTERM (graceful termination)
• kill -9 1234: send SIGKILL (force kill, use when SIGTERM doesn't work)
• kill -15 1234: send SIGTERM explicitly
• killall [process_name]: kill all processes with that name

pgrep [pattern] - Find process IDs by pattern
pkill [pattern] - Kill processes matching a pattern

Background and foreground:
command & - Run command in background (returns to prompt)
ctrl+Z - Suspend current process
bg - Resume suspended process in background
fg - Bring background process to foreground
jobs - List background processes

nohup [command] - Run command that continues after logout
screen or tmux - Create persistent terminal sessions

nice [priority] [command] - Start process with specific priority
renice [priority] [PID] - Change priority of running process
• Lower values = higher priority (-20 to 19)

sections.title: Networking Commands
sections.content:
ping [host] - Test connectivity to a host
• ping -c 5 google.com: send 5 packets and stop
• Shows if host is reachable and latency

ifconfig - Display network interface configuration
• Shows IP addresses, MAC addresses, and network stats
• Modern Linux uses 'ip' command instead

ip [options] - Show/manipulate IP configuration
• ip addr: display IP addresses
• ip route: show routing table
• ip link: show network interfaces

nslookup [domain] or dig [domain] - Query DNS servers
• Resolves domain names to IP addresses
• dig provides more detailed DNS information

whois [domain] - Get domain registration information

netstat [options] - Show network statistics and connections
• netstat -an: show all connections and listening ports
• netstat -tulpn: show listening ports with process info
• Modern alternative: ss (socket statistics)

ss [options] - Socket statistics (newer alternative to netstat)
• ss -tulpn: show all listening sockets
• ss -an: show all connections

ssh [user@host] - Secure Shell, remote login to another computer
• ssh user@192.168.1.100: connect to remote host
• ssh -p 2222 user@host: connect to specific port

scp [source] [destination] - Secure copy files between hosts
• scp file.txt user@host:/path/: copy to remote
• scp user@host:/path/file.txt ./: copy from remote

ftp [host] - File Transfer Protocol (less secure, rarely used)
wget [URL] - Download files from web
curl [URL] - Fetch content or make HTTP requests

iptables - Configure firewall rules (advanced)
firewall-cmd or ufw - Firewall management tools

sections.title: Shell Scripting Basics
sections.content:
A shell script is a file containing shell commands executed sequentially. Scripts automate repetitive tasks.

Creating a script:
1. Create file with .sh extension
2. Add shebang at first line: #!/bin/bash
3. Write commands
4. Make executable: chmod +x script.sh
5. Run: ./script.sh

Variables:
VARNAME=value - Assign value (no spaces around =)
$VARNAME - Access variable value
read VARNAME - Read input from user
export VARNAME - Make variable available to child processes

Conditionals:
if [ condition ]; then
  commands
elif [ condition ]; then
  commands
else
  commands
fi

Common conditions:
[ -f file ] - file exists and is regular file
[ -d directory ] - directory exists
[ -x file ] - file is executable
[ "$var" = "text" ] - string equals
[ "$var" != "text" ] - string not equals
[ $num -eq 5 ] - number equals
[ $num -lt 10 ] - less than
[ $num -gt 10 ] - greater than

Loops:
for var in list; do
  commands
done

while [ condition ]; do
  commands
done

Functions:
function_name() {
  commands
  return value
}

function_name param1 param2 - Call function with parameters

Special variables:
$0 - Script name
$1, $2, $3 - First, second, third parameter
$# - Number of parameters
$@ - All parameters
$? - Exit status of last command (0 = success)

User input and output:
echo "text" - Print text
echo -e "line1\nline2" - Print with escape sequences
read -p "Enter name: " name - Prompt user for input

Example script:
#!/bin/bash
echo "Welcome to my script"
read -p "Enter your name: " name
if [ -z "$name" ]; then
  echo "No name provided"
else
  echo "Hello, $name!"
fi
